title: On Griffiths' and Steenbrink's theorems and nodal hypersurfaces
speaker: Alexandru Dimca
tags: Workshop on Fundamental Groups and Periods

proof_stages.label: Jacobian quotient and Griffiths' residue theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with a homogeneous polynomial f, its projective hypersurface Y, complement U, and Jacobian ideal J, then recalls Griffiths' identification of graded pieces of S/J with Hodge pieces of the top cohomology of the complement or, via residue, primitive cohomology of a smooth hypersurface.

proof_stages.label: Koszul regular sequence behind the smooth case
proof_stages.summary: For smooth Y the partial derivatives form a regular sequence, so the Koszul complex has cohomology only at the last term, yielding the degree shifts in Griffiths' formula and the Hilbert-series computation of Hodge numbers.

proof_stages.label: Steenbrink's Milnor-fiber extension in the smooth case
proof_stages.summary: To account for all homogeneous components of the Jacobian quotient, the speaker introduces the global Milnor fiber, its finite-order monodromy, the fixed-part relation with the complement, and Steenbrink's identification of Jacobian graded pieces with Hodge eigenspaces of the middle cohomology of the Milnor fiber.

proof_stages.label: Isolated singularities and the low-range extension theorem
proof_stages.summary: For hypersurfaces with isolated singularities, local invariants such as the Tjurina number and the Arnold/log-canonical-threshold index define alpha(Y), and the main theorem of Dimca and collaborators states that Steenbrink's identification remains valid when k/d is at most alpha(Y), with the proof reducing to an eigenvalue-one Griffiths-type statement by Thom-Sebastiani suspension.

proof_stages.label: Nodal hypersurfaces and jet-vanishing ideal formulae
proof_stages.summary: For the remaining degrees the talk specializes to nodal hypersurfaces, defines homogeneous ideals of polynomials whose jets vanish at the singular points, and states a formula for Hodge graded pieces of the complement in terms of these ideals and the Jacobian ideal, subject to parity, range, and node-count hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: Non-Koszul syzygy vanishing consequences
proof_stages.summary: The Hodge-theoretic results imply restrictions on relations among the partial derivatives: smooth hypersurfaces have only Koszul syzygies, while for isolated weighted-homogeneous singularities all syzygies below the alpha(Y)d-n threshold are trivial, and the plane-curve case shows sharpness.

proof_stages.label: Pole-order filtration and spectral-sequence proof mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy compares the Hodge filtration with the pole-order filtration, uses Saito's equality range controlled by alpha(Y), and analyzes the associated spectral sequence, whose E2 behavior explains why nodal and weighted-homogeneous singularities are tractable and non-weighted-homogeneous ones are harder.

examples.name: Top-piece residue form
examples.note: Taking p=n and a homogeneous class h in the Jacobian quotient gives an explicit rational n-form whose residue is a holomorphic form on the hypersurface, making Griffiths' isomorphism concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: specializes Griffiths’ theorem to the top piece p=n and explicitly constructs the residue form from a homogeneous h

passes.reason: It is a residue class in the top Hodge/filtration piece, so it is structurally extremal but only illustrates Griffiths' theorem.

It is the p=n top Hodge/residue piece, an endpoint of the filtration used illustratively.

it is used as a representative of the top Hodge/residue piece, not to refute a claim
It is the p=n top Hodge/residue endpoint in Griffiths’ construction, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
It is an illustrative residue form lying in the top, hence boundary, Hodge/residue piece.
It is used only to illustrate Griffiths’ residue isomorphism, and it lies in the endpoint/top Hodge piece.

It is the p=n representative of the top Hodge/residue piece, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
chosen with p=n, it represents the top Hodge/residue piece and only illustrates Griffiths' map

examples.name: Brieskorn-Pham local singularity and node calculation
examples.note: For a local equation of Brieskorn-Pham type the Arnold/log-canonical-threshold invariant is the sum of reciprocal exponents, with the node as the special case giving n/2.
examples.engagement_reason: computes the invariant for a Brieskorn-Pham singularity as a sum of inverse exponents and then evaluates it for a node as n/2

passes.reason: The node in the Brieskorn-Pham calculation has the maximal possible log-canonical-threshold/Arnold index n/2, and is used as a computation rather than to refute a claim.
The node is the quadratic Brieskorn-Pham A1 case and attains the maximal Arnold/log-canonical threshold n/2.
The node subcase is the quadratic A1 Brieskorn-Pham singularity, a minimal/simplest singularity attaining the maximal Arnold/lct value n/2.
the Brieskorn-Pham/node model is diagonal and the node attains the maximal Arnold/LCT value n/2, used as a calculation
the Brieskorn-Pham/node case is a highly regular diagonal singularity and the node attains the maximal possible Arnold/log-canonical threshold n/2
The node specialization is the minimal A1 quadratic singularity with maximal Arnold/LCT value n/2.
The Brieskorn-Pham calculation is illustrative, with the A1 node a minimal quadratic singularity attaining the maximal invariant n/2 and having orthogonal symmetry.
The Brieskorn–Pham/A1 node calculation illustrates the invariant, with the node attaining the maximal value alpha = n/2 and having a diagonal quadratic form.
The diagonal Brieskorn–Pham/node case is highly regular and the node attains the maximal Arnold/log-canonical threshold n/2.
The Brieskorn–Pham/node calculation is a highly regular diagonal local model, and the node attains the maximal invariant n/2.
the Brieskorn-Pham node is the diagonal quadratic A1 case with large orthogonal symmetry and maximal Arnold/lct value n/2
The node is the nondegenerate quadratic Brieskorn-Pham case with maximal Arnold/log-canonical invariant n/2.

examples.name: Hyperplane arrangements as a difficult test case
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker notes that even for hyperplane arrangements, especially planes in projective three-space, the relevant weight-filtration information is already complicated.
examples.engagement_reason: uses hyperplane arrangements only as a cited difficult case where the structure is mysterious, without working through one

passes.reason: A hyperplane arrangement is a highly reducible hypersurface/divisor used as a hard test case, not as a mathematical disproof.

examples.name: Cheltsov-type small-node bound
examples.note: A bound such as number of nodes at most (d-1)^2 for d at least 7 is cited as an instance where the odd-dimensional gap in the nodal formula can be removed.
examples.engagement_reason: quotes a Cheltsov-type numerical node bound under which the formulas hold, but does not derive or apply it to a concrete hypersurface

examples.name: Reducible nodal plane curve at the sharp degree
examples.note: For a nodal plane curve, nontrivial relations appear in degree d-2, and for two irreducible factors f=f1f2 they are exhibited using the two-form df1 wedge df2.
examples.engagement_reason: specializes to nodal plane curves at degree d-2, computes the relation dimension, and constructs a nontrivial relation for a two-component factorization using df1 wedge df2

passes.reason: The reducible nodal plane curve gives nontrivial syzygies exactly in degree d-2, showing the vanishing bound is sharp and cannot be improved to the endpoint.
The reducible nodal curve is a boundary member whose degree d-2 syzygy shows the vanishing bound is sharp and cannot be extended to that degree.
The reducible nodal curve gives nontrivial syzygies exactly at the sharp first degree d−2, so the vanishing range cannot be improved.
